draft.introduction: The opening passage of the Analects establishes the three pillars of Confucian life: the joy of learning, the warmth of friendship, and the inner composure of the superior person who does not depend on recognition.

translation.text: The Master said: "Is it not a pleasure to learn and to practice what one has learned from time to time? Is it not a joy to have friends come from afar? Is it not the mark of the superior person to feel no resentment even when others fail to recognize one's merits?"
translation.original_text: 子曰：学而时习之，不亦说乎？有朋自远方来，不亦乐乎？人不知而不愠，不亦君子乎？

translator_notes.note: The word xue (learning) in Confucian usage means not merely intellectual study but the active cultivation of virtue through practice, ritual, and self-examination. The junzi (superior person, gentleman) is not an aristocratic title but a moral achievement—anyone can become one through sustained effort.
